posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I was having dinner with friends last week, and the subject of Jayer came up. I mentioned that I had never had one, and not sure I ever will, but maybe one day… I have a few questions for those who have been able to drink his wines.

  1. What did he do that was so special?
  2. Why can’t it be replicated?
  3. Which producers are making wine that are close to his in style and quality?
  4. Is his Cros Parentoux the most Jayer of Jayers?

I’ve been reading about Jayer quite a lot in the last month and have been fascinated by what he was doing.

I think Jayer’s wines are still (somewhat) accessible. Depends on if you want his own labeled stuff or if you want to try it via Pre-2002 Emmanuel Rouget (where he apparently told/showed Rouget what to do every step of the way) or Pre-89 Meo-Camuzet.

That being said, I haven’t had any yet myself. Cros-P from him seems to be the unique one, but his GCs (Echezeaux and Richebourg) are supposed to be outstanding.

Looking forward to more experienced members on here chiming in.

posts.name: Berry Crawford
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:
  1. What did he do that was so special?

Ive never tried any of his wines, but reading about the history it seems he put emphasis on low yields and precision farming when most of his peers were not. Also, I think he may have been ahead of the curve in heavy use of new oak. So an emphasis on good, low yield farming and heavy oak would have made him stand out.

Mark Golodetz:
  1. Why can’t it be replicated?

Every wine maker ends up having their own signature. There are so many variables in viticulture and winemaking that its natural that he would have a unique stylistic profile.

I’m would be curious to hear what you find as a consumer of his wines to be unique about them. Ive asked people in the past what they thought was singular about the style and never really heard a clear answer.

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked:
I’ve had some, both the ‘real’ Jayer and the Meo version and the pre '02 Rouget version. Though I’m sure many on this board have had 10x or 100x my experience level.

  1. An early advocate of destemming and ripe fruit and low yields. Also great and serious vineyard work. And he used a very reasonably amount of new oak. If someone today said “I’m de-stemming, picking ripe and using lots of oak”, they’d be vilified.

  2. It can be and is, of course, but like when people say that Oscar Robertson or Michael Jordan or Magic Johnson was/is much better than Lebron James, it’s because of what people have in their mind, not really fact.

  3. I’ll say Perrot-Minot or maybe Dugat-Py, or maybe even Dominique Laurent. Flameproof suit on! Really a mixture of any of these and also Leroy (for the very low yields and great vineyard management)

  4. I guess so as he reclaimed this vineyard from artichokes and rocks or whatever it was. So the only vineyard that was completely ‘Jayer’ produced…

posts.name: L e o F r o k i c
posts.cooked:
He was the first to start using cold soak maceration before fermentation, later popularize by oenologist Guy Accad. He never performed saignee and would always destem grapes along with 100% new oak for 18months. No wood vats for fermentation for same reason as 100%new oak, not to impart any off flavors which he claims had destroyed many wines post second world war when Burgundy wines didn’t sell so well and barrels were constantly reused. No filtration and fining only with egg whites.

Also worth reading here is the Henri Jayer Archives - Winehog - Burgundy with a passion
posts.display_username: L e o F r o k i c

link_counts.title: Henri Jayer Archives - Winehog - Burgundy with a passion

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
Mark - have you read or are you reading “A Tribute to the Great Wines of Burgundy” by Jacky Rigaux and translated by Jim Finkel? That should fill in a lot of gaps for you.

I have only had a few Jayer ('86/'98 CP) and Rouget ('92/'95 Eche) wines and they were very good, big, bold wines. Nothing feminine about them. Pricing sucks.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
His wines are opulent and sexy. He made incredibly good wines in bad vintages. It is hard to dismiss the hype much like Gentaz and Foucault, ie very very good but only a few great ones. Having said that the 85 Cros Parantoux drank ten years ago is one of the top five wines of my life. For my palate, the closest wines are Comte Liger Belair and Cathiard.

I had a dinner at a two star restaurant in Dijon in the late 90s. I popped my Jayer cherry with the 94 H Jayer VR village for 70 euros. It was so good that the next day I went for a lunch and drank the 94 Cros Parantoux for 170 euros.

I am aware of a Korean guy who sold alot of fake Jayers in Korea and now lives in NYC.
Kevin, how long ago was that? Those prices… !

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
AlbertH:

Kevin, how long ago was that? Those prices… !

It was a while back. I still remember two mags of the 78 H Jayer Echezeaux selling for under $2k at Butterfields & Butterfields and thinking the price was outrageous in the late 90s.

Cool. Awesome that you got the opportunity especially at that price point.

Yes, going on wine-searcher and looking at the trend over the years is depressing. Short of some very very special event, I don’t forsee myself purchasing any Henri Jayer in the near future. Will have to bring something great to a Le Paulee event and hope to get a taste in return perhaps!

posts.name: John J. Vachon
posts.cooked: I’ve only had two both 1982’s (a lesser year) but great wine.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: note that his great niece, Cecile Tremblay, is an outstanding winemaker in Burgundy (winery in Morey St. Denis). Cannot really compare her wines or methods to his as I have not had enough of his wines, but her wines are really outstanding.

posts.name: Al1 M ï c h ě l
posts.cooked:
^ Unfortunately, for someone seeking better/reasonnable prices, this won’t help ! Prices are very high imo for such a young producer. But, it seems that she indeed does great wines. Never had a chance to check for myself though.

Alain
posts.display_username: Al1 M ï c h ě l

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
The only Jayer wine I´ve ever had the opportunity to taste was a small glass of the 1987 Vosne-Romanee (Village) - but god, what a wine for a village (and the vintage)!
A perfect combination of the most elegant complex fruit with intensity but without any weight, blind could easily have been an Echezeaux, and then (12-13 years ago) in a perfect state …

First heir sure is Emmanuel Rouget, he makes stunning wines even from lesser vineyards … great balance usually …
I do see some similarities also in Liger-Belairs wines (Comte) … but Meo for instance is quite different, although I have no comparison for the Cros Parantoux with Jayer …

There are many more who try to use some of Jayers techniques, but the results count …

Also Sylvain Cathiard is (was) in that direction, although the oak is more obvious in the youth (than at Rouget) …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Faryan Amir-Ghassemi
posts.cooked:
2000 Crox Parantoux (E. Rouget) changed my appreciation for Burgundy. Alas, the price!

Great stories Kevin.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked:
Very passionate and well known for advocating de-stemming. He was known to ask, would you ever eat stem by itself? He was also known as a top farmer.

Not so much red, but maybe the closest producer going is Coche?

Fourrier learned from Jayer I believe but I’m sure there’s a lot lost in translation as well as a change in the times.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: Ie had a Jayer and a few Rouget and Meo pre 89. All were good but nowhere near the hype let alone price. I believe he was ahead of his time but now burgundy has caught up. Last thing, i wouldnt consider a bottle now without full proof provenance. Not oh my friend boughtthis 10 years ago. More like my dad bought it from XYZ and here’s the receipt. Easiest label to copy.

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked:
I have a single Jayer left in my cellar (not counting 2002 and earlier Rouget!)

1988 Jayer ‘Parantoux’. While the auction value is nuts ($5k??), I’m holding onto it as my ‘ticket’ into some crazy high end tasting in the near or far future…

posts.name: Berry Crawford
posts.cooked:
Robert Pavlovich:

He was known to ask, would you ever eat stem by itself? .

I wonder if anyone asked him if he would eat an oak barrel?

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked:
I think the reply was “would you eat salt by itself”?

Heard about the exchange on an IDTT podcast with one of the Wasserman’s.

I get what Jayer was saying though, as material other than grapes, but good point about the oak.

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: I’ve had several vintages of Jayer, most recently the 86 & 88 Cros side by side, and the 80 Echezeaux. No one has the purity of fruit that he has in his wines. His attention to all details in the wine making process was second to none.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Ten days ago, i’m sitting at the table with, let’s say, some young wine professionals in Burgundy over a home cooked stew. A mag of ‘13 NSG villages first, pretty tasty, primary, very well made. Then a mag served blind. First whiff, three of the 5 heads at the table are saying, meh, not so good. Tasty taste. The host and I are silent. I think that it is at the very least an interesting wine, got a lil’ sun’in’ to say, but by no means terribly inspiring. In a “hey this glass is half full” kind of way I say as much.
Reveal.
Rouget Parantoux ‘11 in magnum.
Discussion ensues, with the consensus opinion that the Rouget wines are often sort of meh. Beaumonts and Echezeaux are more regularly inspiring than the Cros Parantoux, i’m told. The Savigny can be quite good, i’m told.
As happens on a regular basis in this tiny circle, youtube videos of Rouget talking in his cellar are watched with oh-my-god-this-is-too-easy hilarity, his mannerisms naturally, well, funny.
It is so very wonderfully refreshing when the hype and fetishism is all stripped away, and it comes back to friends sharing food and wine, without pretension, open to the simple candor of “this is just sort of meh” about something that too often gets contorted into a weird little fetishy world of speculators and trophy seekers chasing some mythic Jayer dragon at stooopid prices.
Friends exchanging wines, drinking wines. Keepin’ it real. Even if you’ve got Jayer in your veins.
:0

posts.name: Harry Gruber
posts.cooked: Kermit Lynch was one of the first (if not THE first) to bring Jayer to the United States. I remember being seduced by his newsletter for the first (IIRC) vintage he imported, the 1978s, and sprung for a 6 pack of the village Vosne, which was as much as I could afford in those days. (By today’s standards, the asking price was ridiculously cheap.) I drank them all way too soon but they were epiphany Burgs. For me, they were much like Gerhard P. described his experience in post 14, above, but with a true velvet feel to the body, and a never-ending finish. The nose was opulent Asian spicebox, and it was a joy to look at in the glass. In hopes of recreating that experience, I ran across a single bottle of the 1987 NSG “Les Meurgers” at Dee Vine in the early 2000s, and treated myself to it during the Christmas holidays. The explosion in Jayer prices had not yet taken place, and the bottle cost me (IIRC) around $220. Despite the inauspicious vintage, the wine was still magical. It had shed whatever baby fat it came with and was a weightless wonder. I still remember those 78 Vosne village wines, though. Absolutely incredible stuff!

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
FWIW

  • 1985 Henri Jayer Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru Cros Parantoux - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Vosne-Romanée 1er Cru (1/25/2005)
    I have never had any wine with such purity. The color is so brilliant that it glows like a diamond. The nose is so intense and pure, utterly indescribable. Wild fire, Tornado of wine, constant sensory overload, wave after wave of perfectly ripe dark and red berries, cold smoked rare beef blood, beet, aged balsamico essence, clove, wet limestone and etc. In the mouth, unbelievably intensity yet feels like a cotton candy as the wine is perfectly balanced and delineated. May I dare say that this wine had twice the concentration of the 85 La Tahce. Perfectly focused core fruit with complementing mineral quality, a red wine equivalent of a great bottle of les clos. The finish is unbelievably long and so sweet, Wow Wow Wow. Unlike the 85 La Tache, the nose did not change/evolve at all throughout the night. In my personal preference, this really was twice better than the 85 La Tache. I would never thought that there can be anything better than the 62 La Tache but this bottle changed my mind. (100 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

Robert Panzer:

Ten days ago, i’m sitting at the table with, let’s say, some young wine professionals in Burgundy over a home cooked stew. A mag of ‘13 NSG villages first, pretty tasty, primary, very well made. Then a mag served blind. First whiff, three of the 5 heads at the table are saying, meh, not so good. Tasty taste. The host and I are silent. I think that it is at the very least an interesting wine, got a lil’ sun’in’ to say, but by no means terribly inspiring. In a “hey this glass is half full” kind of way I say as much.
Reveal.
Rouget Parantoux '11 in magnum.
Discussion ensues, with the consensus opinion that the Rouget wines are often sort of meh.

No, it’s just a 2011 that may (or may not be!) compromised, of-course depending on your mileage
Having just had the opportunity to retaste, most of the 11 Comte Liger-Belair’s are the same now the oak has faded (a bit!)

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked: My understanding is that Rouget’s ’09, ’10 and 12’s are incredibly good. Unfortunately the grey market price reflects that.

posts.name: scott c
posts.cooked:
Other people have already spoken about Jayer’s methods in the vineyards and cellar. The only other thing I think I’ve heard was that he was a strong proponent of tight row spacing/vine density.

As for the wine style, the only Jayer I’ve ever had was the 2001 Cros Parantoux, generously shared by Jim Clendenen at La Paulee a couple years ago. In a night of dozens of trophy wines, it left an impression for sure. Sexy and masculine, but with layers upon layers of complex and precise aromas and flavors – pure red fruit, exotic spices, and cool minerality. What I remember most, though, is the mouthfeel – the lightness of the wine on the palate combined with the intensity of flavor – a seeming paradox that I’ve only experienced a few times with great Burgundy (the first being a 1990 Leroy Beaux Monts if you’re looking for a stylistic comparison).

Just last weekend, we did a three-vintage vertical of Rouget Cros Parantoux, including the 2001, which was as close as I’ve gotten to replicating that experience. (We also had the '99 and '02, neither of which was showing much and need tons of time.) Obviously, Jayer was still around and helping/showing his nephew or even making the wines depending on who you ask, so I can’t speak about whether post-2002 Rougets are any different. But, for me, older Rougets are stunning examples of classic iron fist/velvet glove red Burgundy for a fraction of the cost of H. Jayer wines (still not cheap, of course). I can still smell that '01 from last week.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I had a Rouget Echezeaux 2001 recently with a friend as well as the Dujac Combottes from the same vintage. This does not sound in any way similar to the Jayers described here.

WineBerserkers – 3 Jun 15

TN: Dauvissat, Dujac and Rouget

A friend from Brazil came in for lunch, and we decided to open a few Burgundies. First off, a lovely Dauvissat Les Clos 2000. Showing slight signs of age, it was at the same time complex with white peach, iodine, honeycomb and a quite lovely...

posts.name: scott c
posts.cooked:
Mark Golodetz:

I had a Rouget Echezeaux 2001 recently with a friend as well as the Dujac Combottes from the same vintage. This does not sound in any way similar to the Jayers described here.

TN: Dauvissat, Dujac and Rouget - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

Sounds like it needed a lot of air/is still very young. Our '01 Cros P was double decanted most of an entire day. Or it could be explained by any multitude of other reasons – terroir, provenance, fruit day vs root day – whatever! Wine is hard.

posts.name: Ralph Earle
posts.cooked:
Peter Hirsch:

I have a single Jayer left in my cellar (not counting 2002 and earlier Rouget!)

1988 Jayer ‘Parantoux’. While the auction value is nuts ($5k??), I’m holding onto it as my ‘ticket’ into some crazy high end tasting in the near or far future…

+1 (except for the “far future” part, 'cause I’m 72), a 1971 Echezeaux ($10K?!?!)

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Ralph, buy that wine or your children will spend it on something else!

I knew Henri, visited three or four times, since he bought his barrels from us. One time Jean Francois and I had just finished delivering a small barrel to DRC and Henri flagged us down. We were forced to spend the afternoon drinking with him.

I do know that he said things that were misleading. Every time a winemaker called us and asked for light or medium toast I asked him how his trip to Jayer went. I saw his barrels being made…heavy toast. Spacing etc in the Cote d Or is pretty well regulated. I don’t think he could have gone 8 x 12.

His wines were unique and God only knows what he really did.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Jerry Hey:

I’ve had several vintages of Jayer, most recently the 86 & 88 Cros side by side, and the 80 Echezeaux. No one has the purity of fruit that he has in his wines. His attention to all details in the wine making process was second to none.

Yeah, this.

Never seemed to make a bad wine, even in tough years.

I’ve had dozens of his wines (no Richebourg though, sadly), and whilst the 1’er’s are very good (and can be great), they aren’t worth the $$$$'s, especially older wines, and especially at the crazy price levels now.

The Cros is a different beast though. So obviously high GC quality, and needing a lot of time to show their best. Better than the Ech? Not really so sure about that.

The Ech. is the sweet spot wine for mine, they are pure, complex, layered and beautiful wines.

Funnily enough the late 1990’s-early 2000 Jayers seem to be his lesser wines. Don’t seem to have the magic (for me) of the late '70’s -early '90’s wines…

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
I had many Jayers from most vintages . One of my best wine-friends used to visit Jayer back in the seventies and bought direct all the way till he retired . I think his allocation was around 5 cases a year , mostly Richebourg , Cros P , Echezeaux , VR Brulees etc . We get together about 6 times a year and most of the times , he brings a Jayer . My friend also has a bad memory , 3 times in a row he brought a Richebourg 1985 and thought I never had it .
I also attended a tasting in Beaune where we compared Jayer Echezeaux with the one from DRC from about 12 vintages . Very interesting .
So , my take ?

  • Jayer excels in so called poor vintages like 1980 . I am not a fan of Jayer’s wines in very ripe vintages like 1990 and 1999 where I find his wines too sweet .
  • Jayer always destems completely . I think that is a mistake in vintages where the stems are not green . Stems add complexity when they are not green ( That’s why I prefer the DRC wines in those vintages ).
  • Jayer picked relatively early and always chaptalized . Again , that’s good in less ripe vintages , but it should not be in ripe vintages .
  • there is a Jayer style : very pure , deceptively light but very intense , beautifully crafted wines that age for ever . The wines that come closest to Jayer imo are the wines from Jacky Truchot . Most of the wines from Truchot are sought after and bought with great passion in the US , so this is not a big secret unfortunately .
  • I agree with Jon , the books by Jacky Rigaud are a must read if you want to understand Jayer . Rigaud also wrote another small book with an overview of all the known vintages of Burgundy with comments of each vintage by Jayer . I love that book .

The most amazing wine from Jayer was not a Richebourg or a Cros Parantoux but the Echezeaux 1980 from magnum . The finesse in that wine was mind boggling .

I think his allocation was around 5 cases a year , mostly Richebourg , Cros P , Echezeaux , VR Brulees etc . We get together about 6 times a year and most of the times , he brings a Jayer . My friend also has a bad memory , 3 times in a row he brought a Richebourg 1985 and thought I never had it .
I also attended a tasting in Beaune where we compared Jayer Echezeaux with the ones from DRC from about 12 vintages . Very interesting .
So , my take ?

  • Jayer excels in so called poor vintages like 1980 . I am not a fan of Jayer’s wines in very ripe vintages like 1990 and 1999 where I find his wines too sweet .
  • Jayer always destems completely . I think that is a mistake in vintages where the stems are not green . Stems add complexity when they are not green ( That’s why I prefer the DRC wines in those vintages ).
  • Jayer picked relatively early and always chaptalized . Again , that’s good in less ripe vintages , but it should not be in ripe vintages .
  • there is a Jayer style : very pure , deceptively light but very intense , beautifully crafted wines that age for ever . The wines that come closest to Jayer imo are the wines from Jacky Truchot . Most of the wines from Truchot are sought after and bought with great passion in the US , so this is not a big secret unfortunately .
  • I agree with Jon , the books by Jacky Rigaud are a must read if you want to understand Jayer . Rigaud also wrote another small book with an overview of all the known vintages of Burgundy with comments of each vintage by Jayer . I love that book .

The most amazing wine from Jayer was not a Richebourg or a Cros Parantoux but the Echezeaux 1980 from magnum . The finesse in that wine was mind boggling .

Fascinating comments, Herwig, thank you!

+1

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
I’ve only had most of the mid 80s vintages - heavy in 1984 and 1985 when I was a retailer (and was able to get one case of his '85 wines for every case of '84 that I bought) -

The upper tier stuff was of course incredible - but I will never, ever forget the 1985 Bourgogne Rouge from Henri Jayer - easily the best Bourgogne Rouge I have ever tasted, we had a couple cases that came in that were heat damaged, so the importer replaced them, and I of course drank those ‘heat damaged’ bottles over a time. His wines are just so vivid, so precise, with that gorgeous crushed red berry essence in even the most youthful of his releases. I still remember them like it was yesterday -

I also have not had enough experience with Jayer to make the comparison. I do note that from what I understand yields were a huge issue for Jayer, while I understand that Truchot never really tried to keep yields down, although he did have the advantage of having old vines.

Philippe Engel used to rave about the great wine cellars in Belgium.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked:
Here is a bit from a Jayer article in Burghound #13. (Allen Meadows has posted it publicly on boards a few times in the past so no copyright issues.)

The Jayer Method: Much has been made of Jayer’s winemaking approach and as I began to ask questions, Jayer really warmed to his subject. This isn’t the place for an in-depth discussion of the Jayer method but in brief, Jayer describes it as follows: “Prune short and ruthlessly sort. There is no substitute for low yields and anyone who says otherwise is kidding themselves. I never did like chemicals and I insisted on plowing to remove weeds and I learned that my vines were stronger because plowing cuts the surface roots. Anti-rot treatments were introduced in 1980 and they work pretty well as long as one uses them only when necessary. And no, we don’t do any bio-dynamic farming, which in my view is blatant idiocy. As to the winemaking, we cold macerate for up to a week depending on the vintage but we’re not Accad in either degree or intent as we use far less SO2. I ferment, completely naturally of course, until the sugar is gone and then rack into 100% new oak barrels that I order with medium toast. I have always chosen my barrels by smell alone and the provenance of the wood was never a high priority; it’s the treatment that counts. I also never pre-treat my barrels either; no rinsing, no salt solutions, no nothing. I am extremely careful during the élevage as here is where the quality of the wine can be lost or diminished through sloppy practices. After 18 months in a typical vintage, we bottle by hand and cask by cask. Until 1990, I used to fine with egg whites but I was always adamantly against filtering. I want to make sure that my wines taste as good in bottle as they do from barrel so whenever possible, I let the wines make themselves. In fact, nothing in the world is easier than making wine”.

I remarked to Jayer that his method was logical but that there was nothing unique in what he was saying. I also noted that he had freely shared his approach with any number of aspiring young winemakers yet no one had yet really approached the quality of the Jayer wines, at least not consistently. So, I had to ask, what was the secret? Jayer, ever modest yet quietly proud of his accomplishments, simply said, “well, there might be a secret or two that I haven’t mentioned. But much more important than a small trick or two is that you have to feel your wines. There’s an art to it and that’s something that you can’t teach anymore than a painter or musician can teach a student by showing him how to draw or read music. In short, it’s a gift.” However, he was quick to point out that anyone can do quality élevage, observing “how many wines do you see that are prematurely brown? Too many. Have you ever seen one of my wines, when properly stored, that has turned? And the reason is that I treat all of my wines, from the Bourgogne to the Cros as though they were babies in need of tender loving care. I never pump and I never expose them to oxygen except during a racking and the hand bottling never upsets their equilibrium.”

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Thanks Keith.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Jerry Hey:

Interesting comment from Herwig on the 80 Echezeaux. The bottle I had was bought on release from Kermit for some insanely cheap price and served with a 1985 DRC Grand Echezeaux and a 1961 Leroy Grand Echezeaux, and the 80 Jayer Echezeaux was clearly the best of that flight. Amazing purity…

I’ve had the '80 Ech. a couple of times also (including from mag once - it’s my wifes birthyear) and also found it to be an excelent wine. Sadly, only one bottle left.

Love to try it next to some '80 DRC’s as an interesting comparrison. Jayer Ech Vs DRC in general is an interesting discussion in stlylistic preference Vs vintage…


The '85 Ech. is probably my favourite of his wines overall, although they seemed to have a high rate of cork taint issues.

I thought Jayers’ '87’s were also excellent wines for the vintage, the 1’ers were readilly available for a very reasonable price, and were the best '87’s I have seen.

Last two bottles I had (the last two left) were both a bit faded though. His '86’s were similarly very good, and have had good '79’s, '82’s and '83’s.

I have also found his '90’s to be excellent, maybe better even than '90 DRC from the small sample I have had, so a slightly different view to Herwigs.

As I said before though, I am not such a big fan of his later wines though, and was pretty nonplussed with the '99’s I had also (maybe these just need much more time).

The G and H Jayer and George Jayer wines are clearly a step down, and the Rouget’s I find to sometimes be a bit hit and miss (more hit than miss though), but can still be excellent, whilst the Jayer-Gilles we have had (especially some of the older ones) were surprisingly good!

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Some thoughts on this subject. We can all read what Jayer did. And, IMO, if we want to get experience with “Jayer” wines, find Emmanuel Rouget’s, age them and analyze them. Rouget will tell you he has and will not “change” anything Jayer taught him.

Jayer’s main contribution was/is that he was a thinker before people thought about winemaking methods and, particularly, hygeine in small Burgundy domaines. He was a trained enology graduate in years where the people heading most domaine’s couldn’t spell the word. (Does it start with one of those conjoined o and e or not?) And, he was a clever businessman…he saw opportunity in the post world war II Burgundy scene and he took advantage of it: reconditioned and re-claimed the Cros P and worked deals on other great vineyards owned by families (the Meo and Camuzet families) that were gentry who needed such help. And, he tied them up cleverly for longterm (most were tied up till his own “retirement” age – though he never “retired”.)

He had a plan and an understanding of modern practices…good and bad that the generation of post war baby boomers implemented in the '80s when they took over family domaines. Only he had that understanding when their fathers were in charge and probably didn’t care to think about them.

I think that’s why we admire/esteem the man so much. (And the market charges so much.) And, the wines all taste good that I’ve had, even if maybe not the best value (anytime Kermit Lynch added his tithe, to me, value was iffy.) And, they are the product of clean clean winemaking. His place in Vosne looked like you should go outside to sneeze. (And when my then 7 year old climbed on top of a pile of barrels, the guy was purple.) He was meticululous in his thinking and his practices.

Frankly, having visited him and Truchot (many times), the only thing I think the wines have in common is that they are pinot noir. Jacky Truchot was a polar opposite: untrained, rudimentary, no real game plan (during my first visit, in 1985, he opened a mystery bottle; it was terrific; he asked us to guess what it was; when we did; he said he had no idea what it was himself; he wasn’t kidding, either.) Jacky was gregarious and didn’t take him self super seriously. Jayer was very formal and focused. Love Jacky’s wines…nevertheless. (Have a 1983 lined up to commemorate the 30th anniversary of that first visit, with the same friend who was with me and my wife (still a close friend). Just hope Jacky put the right label on the bottle.)

And, FWIW, I think the real star of the stable is the Echezaux that Rouget “made” from combined holdings. And the real QPR is the VR Beaux Monts. To me, the Cros P is the like the Rousseau CSJ: more epitomizing its maker in the marketplace than superior quality to other wines in that stable: ie, the trademark wine for the maker.

Not sure what the question really is…but always happy to ramble on.

and, Paul…I don’t think they did all that well in '99 for some reason. I have suspected that the cropping might have been too high, as the wines seems to lack concentration when I tasted them in barrel. (And, Jayer-Gilles is not really a related place, though Georges and Lucien were Henri’s brothers, and he made wine for them.) I don’t think that there is any doubt that, until he died, Jayer “made” the Rouget wines-- though I suspect that he did stop then. (As early as 1988, Jayer claimed to have “retired” and directed people (us) to visit his wife’s nephew, Rouget. But, in 1992, Jayer wasn’t pretending to be retired.)

IMO, the winemaker who most takes after Jayer is Patrice Rion in Premeaux. Both, were constantly trying to improve and were/are always questioning things in every vintage…in the cuverie and in the fields. To me, their styles have long been similar, too, though Jayer’s vineyard holdings much better. (And, ironically, while Jayer was blasting the Cros P. free after the WAR, Daniel Rion, Patrice’s father, was blasting the neighboring “Hauts Beaumonts” in Vosne (now labeled “Beaumonts”). I think there was a friendship, too. )And, as Herwig describes, both, IMO, are the masters of “off” vintages…and suffer by comparison in the “great” vintages, as their tinkering doesn’t add as much. (The WS once wrote an article comparing Patrice Rion and Jayer…maybe in the '80s or early '90s??)

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
Hmm.

Thought Jayer-Gilles was a cousin(?), but the wines are still pretty good either way…

Not sure I get the '99 over-cropping thing. Every '99 I have had from release to recent, the wines are so stacked, I just don’t see the lack of concentration in the wine as an issue, if anything they have too much…

I don’t remember the '99’s as being too ripe or sweet either, just very dark and deep, and perhaps just very closed.

I am a fan of both Rouget’s Ech. and his Vosne. The Cros I am a bit 50/50 on between great and only ok, but would really rather have 2-3 Ech instead.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked: Appreciated stories (laughs!) and insight, Stuart

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Great post Stuart, thanks for sharing.

posts.name: Ian Amstad
posts.cooked:
I tried the 1985 Echezeaux in the early 1990s at a tasting next to the DRC Echezeaux 1985. The Jayer was very primary and ever so pure. The DRC was more accessible, but they were evenly matched.

In the early 2000s I foolishly opened my single bottle of 1987 Echezeaux which was in that awkward adolescent phase that some red burgundies go through.

In the late 2000s I tried the 1996 Cros Parantoux, and it was wine of the night at an extravagant dinner in NYC, where most of wines were served en mag, some of them Bordeaux first growths from the 1940s. For me and I suspect many others the Jayer was the wotn, just edging a 1996 Rousseau Chambertin. It was everything you could want in a red burgundy, with a bit if extra magic to set it apart from the Rousseau. It was still youthful, and would imagine still on the upslope today.

However it didn’t have the kaleidoscopic complexity of the DRCs RC and La Tache 1980s and 1985s.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
k s h i n:

His wines are opulent and sexy. He made incredibly good wines in bad vintages. It is hard to dismiss the hype much like Gentaz and Foucault, ie very very good but only a few great ones. Having said that the 85 Cros Parantoux drank ten years ago is one of the top five wines of my life. For my palate, the closest wines are Comte Liger Belair and Cathiard.

I had a dinner at a two star restaurant in Dijon in the late 90s. I popped my Jayer cherry with the 94 H Jayer VR village for 70 euros. It was so good that the next day I went for a lunch and drank the 94 Cros Parantoux for 170 euros.

I am aware of a Korean guy who sold a lot of fake Jayers in Korea and now lives in NYC.

These wines are texturally perfect. They are so sexy and the balance will slay you. You can’t dismiss the hype here. Unfortunately, now the wines are unobtainable financially. He was a genius at elevage and also in the vineyard. He totally destemmed. It certainly doesn’t always taste that way. (Louis Michel does the same. His wines taste so complex you would swear they were whole bunch.)

Jim Finkel’s translation of Jacky Rigaud’s book really is great in detailing much of the history. The closest are Liger-Belair and Cathiard to me also. On this for sure, Kevin and I agree. The texture is what is very difficult to reproduce.

Phillipe Engel’s wines had this texture but his grandfather was Jayer’s teacher at Oenology school so that is not a surprise that his wines were very similar in many ways. FWIW.
